title: Considered — the ideas we looked at and did NOT model (yet)

preamble.md:
*GENERATED from `inputs/considered.json` — edit the JSON, then run
`mars ledgers` to regenerate this view. Do not hand-edit this file.*

*A register so collaborators know omissions are decisions, not blind spots.
Statuses: **deferred** (on the task list), **out of scope** (real, but not
this tool's job yet), **rejected** (failed verification or graded
low-rigor — do not resurrect without new evidence), **simplified** (modeled,
but with a stated shortcut).*

rows.idea: Phobos/Deimos propellant depots (refuel in Mars orbit, ~½ the surface-lift propellant)
rows.why_where_recorded: Needs a crewed-return / orbital-logistics module the tool doesn't have; revisit when return flights are modeled. COMPARATIVES addendum.

rows.idea: DSN / optical-comms bandwidth limits (Psyche-style laser links)
rows.why_where_recorded: Ground-segment capacity, not a mass/schedule quantity in this model.

rows.idea: SpaceX in-housing matrix (who builds what by 2028)
rows.why_where_recorded: Organizational forecast, not a mass/schedule input. Useful context only.

rows.idea: One-way / no-return crew doctrine
rows.why_where_recorded: Would remove the propellant-banked crew gate and harden life-support requirements instead; a doctrine fork we've deliberately not taken.

rows.idea: Development (non-recurring) cost ledger
rows.why_where_recorded: Recurring launch+cargo cost is modeled; R&D ledger (Starship $15B+, fission, ISRU) still excluded on purpose.

rows.idea: Musk cadence/timeline figures (5→20→100→500 ships; 25–30 yr self-sufficiency; 10–15 windows; late-2026 first flight; ~5-ship first batch as a *plan*)
rows.why_where_recorded: Every one failed 3-vote adversarial verification or was superseded by the Feb-2026 Moon pivot. CITY_RAMP_RESEARCH §4; raw votes in `_city_ramp_raw_findings.json`. Citable as aspiration only.

rows.idea: "600 kW vs 25 kW = demo-to-production ISRU power gap" framing
rows.why_where_recorded: Numbers real, framing wrong: it's linear propellant-mass scaling between two vehicle sizes, killed twice by independent skeptic panels.

rows.idea: arxiv 2112.06145 & 1904.01389 (1000-person colony papers) as anchors
rows.why_where_recorded: Direct reading: qualitative / low-rigor; no extractable budgets. The million-person sibling (2112.06155) survived at Tier C.

rows.idea: 500–1,000 / 5,000–10,000 population thresholds
rows.why_where_recorded: Source auto-graded unreliable; the milestones we carry (110 / 1,000 / 1M) trace to Salotti, NSS, Handmer instead.

rows.idea: NSS's Mars-born-generation criterion for `settlement_established`
rows.why_where_recorded: We gate on population ≥1,000 only; no birth/demographics model exists. Noted in `city.json`.

rows.idea: Orbital assets as a distinct mission type (deploy to orbit, never land)
rows.why_where_recorded: Penetrators/orbital sats are modeled as manifest mass and appear in "surface" inventory; a true `orbital:` mission type is future work if orbital logistics grow.

rows.idea: Ratio-based industrial closure (plant capacity per capita, not presence)
rows.why_where_recorded: Closure stages unlock on component presence + population thresholds; real closure would scale plant *count* with population. Import-decay stages are interpolated between Handmer's two verified endpoints (10 t → 20 kg).

rows.idea: Local-construction mass offsets (NSS: build habitats from regolith after Gen-3 closure)
rows.why_where_recorded: City windows still import all habitat mass; closing structures locally would cut the largest import line. Natural B-phase follow-up.

rows.idea: Population departures / return flights reducing headcount
rows.why_where_recorded: Population only accumulates; crew rotation and returnees aren't modeled.

rows.idea: LH2 boil-off physics for the h2_import mode
rows.why_where_recorded: The mode's weak point (surface LH2 cryocooling) will carry a hard D-tier flag when built.

rows.idea: Probabilistic EDL + two-ship loss
rows.why_where_recorded: edl.py: per-ship P(land) ramping 0.5→0.95 over synods; per-capability survival (independent-loss approximation); rule-of-three demonstrated reliability; loss_tolerance gains n_lost for k-ship. `mars edl` report.

rows.idea: Within-window arrival *sequencing* (early test articles, relay-first, valuable cargo late)
rows.why_where_recorded: C2 models one uniform P(land) per window, not ship-by-ship arrival order. Sequencing (staggered arrival energies, telemetry-relay-first) is a scheduling layer on top; deferred.

rows.idea: Correlated EDL failures (a bad synod, shared design flaw)
rows.why_where_recorded: Survival math assumes independent ship losses. A common-cause failure (dust, a design defect) would correlate them and lower survival; not modeled. The independence assumption is optimistic and flagged in edl.py.

rows.idea: Redundancy-2 is insufficient for multi-component chains at low landing odds
rows.why_where_recorded: return_propellant_proven needs all 5 ISRU components; at p=0.5 qty-2 survives only ~24% (0.75^5). Under no-air-freight the window-0 holds were topped up to qty-3 on the four bottleneck chain steps (elec/CO2/Sabatier/control; cryo stays qty-2 — its 80 kW power drag doesn't fit the margin line) plus doubled single-point sensors (penetrators, recon) → chain survival ~44%, W0 banks 483 t, fleet at 87% mass. The C4 six-ship rejection stands: this buys survival with sunk hold capacity, not extra hulls.

rows.idea: Earth-side readiness gates (orbit, reuse, refill, chill)
rows.why_where_recorded: L1-TRANS requirements, `precondition` verify kind reads per-scenario `transport_readiness` flags; conservative_feasibility opens refill+chill.

rows.idea: The 2037-crew "conservative program" (pre-land everything, crew a window after all gates)
rows.why_where_recorded: `examples/conservative_program.json`. Superseded as baseline by the blue-sky program (crew 2035) after the 2026-07-14 review. Not a rival plan: under `conservative_feasibility` the blue-sky gates hold and crew auto-slips to 2037 — the archive *is* the blue-sky plan's bad-weather outcome.

rows.idea: Tripling fuel capacity on the 2035 crew wave (+4–5 chains, ~8 loads/synod)
rows.why_where_recorded: Packing fact, not preference: +4 chains = 106% of the 20-hull fleet's mass. Double fits at 74%; and the post-2033 3.2 loads/synod already fuels ~3 full evacuations of a 12-person base per synod. Capacity follows demand to the 2037 village wave.

rows.idea: Deep pre-landed consumable caches (90 t+ before crew)
rows.why_where_recorded: Pre-land exactly one synod of survival (45 t bridge, verified before commit); depth flies with the crew at near-zero marginal cost since crew fleets fly light. Same-window provisions are conditioned on the crew's own landing anyway; the bridge hedges the common-mode arrival failure.

rows.idea: Front-loading village-scale hardware onto the half-empty 2035 fleet
rows.why_where_recorded: Violates pilot-one-synod-ahead: the F4 scale buy should be shaped by F3's pilot data, and idle crew-era mass is what the lifecycle review flags. Exception adopted: site-prep rigs moved to 2035 so robots pave the 40-ship landing zone two years ahead.

rows.idea: Return-flight cadence (the scheduled routes home)
rows.why_where_recorded: One Mars→Earth window per synod, first fuel-feasible one ~Jan 2035. Baselined flights: **Jan 2035** uncrewed receipt flight (L1-RET-01; departs before crew, reports during their transit); **Mar 2037** crew #1 return (flies the profile proven 19 months earlier); **May 2039+** rotation era. The ~Nov 2032 window is dead: the pilot plant hasn't banked a load by then. Nothing flies *for* samples — cargo rides routes that exist anyway. Return missions remain unmodeled in the planner (see "Population departures" row).

rows.idea: Return cargo: propellant sample + weathered hardware on the 2035 receipt flight
rows.why_where_recorded: Free mass on a flight that must fly. Note the honest hierarchy: the receipt flight *itself* is the full-fidelity propellant test (the fuel flew a ship home); the tank sample is contaminant forensics, the weathered seals/filters/PV are Earth-lab failure autopsy.

rows.idea: Return cargo: Mars-made materials coupons on the 2037 crew return
rows.why_where_recorded: The one return payload that is load-bearing: alloy ingots, polymer coupons, sintered brick from the 2035 industry pilots, certified in Earth labs (load frames, SEM/CT, ASTM protocols — instrumentation that can never fit the base's mass/labor budget). Results land ~2038, exactly in time to decide whether 2039+ windows substitute local structure for imported habitat tonnage — the largest import line (see "Local-construction mass offsets" row, which this unlocks). Transport loads are a *feature* for coupons: a specimen that survives ascent+cruise+entry then tests to spec has seen worse than service.

rows.idea: Return cargo: regolith / inorganic-chemistry sample returns
rows.why_where_recorded: Perchlorate and regolith chemistry screening flies on flown-heritage wet-chemistry instruments (Phoenix detected perchlorates in situ, 2008). Inorganics aren't altered by transport but gain little from it; on-site answers the operational question immediately vs a 26-month Earth loop.

rows.idea: Return cargo: ice cores / volatiles to Earth
rows.why_where_recorded: Requires cryogenic hold through ascent, cruise, and entry — real engineering for marginal gain over on-site screening. Revisit only if a science customer (not this program) funds it.

rows.idea: Return cargo: agri/plant samples
rows.why_where_recorded: The agri pilots grow crops in actual Mars gravity, radiation, and treated regolith; Earth can only simulate. The pilot is the ground truth — data, not plants, comes home.

rows.idea: Half-empty growth-rule fleets (“the holds are the doctrine” framing)
rows.why_where_recorded: A half-empty hold is an unexamined inherited number, not a virtue. Fleet = max(cargo at ≤90% mass, cumulative-doubling floor); growth-floored windows (2035, 2041) top up with doctrine-clean risk depth. 385 → 322 ships, ~1,070 fewer launches, ~$96 billion saved, landed mass UP 26.5 → 28.4 kt.

rows.idea: Return cargo: organics / astrobiology samples
rows.why_where_recorded: Cruise radiation degrades organics (the known MSR fidelity problem) and life-detection is not this program's driver. A science program's problem, on a science program's budget.

rows.idea: AIAA MGA contingency
rows.why_where_recorded: `aiaa_contingency` scenario: per-group mass-growth allowance replacing the flat 10%; scenario-only pending review.

rows.idea: NASA ISRU plant masses
rows.why_where_recorded: Our chain aggregate validated within 7% of the NASA MAV ISRU study — numbers confirmed, not replaced (PROVENANCE §6).

rows.idea: Semi-Markov component sparing on invented MTBFs
rows.why_where_recorded: A full semi-Markov model on failure rates we'd have to invent is sophistication over guessed data. Instead D4 ships the correct Poisson sparing *method* (`spares.py`, `poisson_spares` scenario) with D-tier ISS-anchored rates, off by default and flagged. Real MTBFs plug in without re-engineering; until then informed_spares (grounded in the 34-41% ECLSS band) is baseline.

rows.idea: Single-hull industry seeds (polymer plant at 2039, electronics fab at 2041) exposed closure milestones to one bad landing
rows.source: found in the post-coupling loss-tolerance sweep
rows.why_where_recorded: The program already buys two of each; the second unit now flies WITH the wave that starts that closure stage instead of a window later (2039 carries both polymer plants, 2041 both electronics fabs), so the pair splits across hulls, and the later window's single unit is backstopped by the one already on the surface. Zero added hardware; fleet, launches, and cost unchanged. Same plant-level-redundancy logic as the 2035 fuel-plant duplication. Every window from 2033 on is now fully single-ship-loss tolerant; the only remaining single-point set is the 2031 habitat+ECLSS demo article, accepted and documented (re-flies in 2033 with ~360 sols of clock margin).

rows.idea: Costing the spares and contingency overheads
rows.source: found during M1-M5 build; re-surfaced adding line-item costs to docs/manifests/
rows.why_where_recorded: Cargo-hardware cost covers manifest items + auto-sized power hardware at catalog unit ranges. Spares (~26% of packed mass) and the mass-growth allowance are carried as MASS only, uncosted: spares pricing would need per-group $/kg assumptions we have no source for, and MGA is a margin, not a purchase. Costing spares at parent-group rates would raise cargo cost roughly a quarter; do it only with a sourced basis. Line items in docs/manifests/ show empty cost cells for spares so the gap is visible, not hidden.

rows.idea: Balanced packer split complementary pairs (habitat vs the ECLSS that runs inside it)
rows.why_where_recorded: Anti-affinity is right for redundant twins, wrong for items that only work together: splitting the demo set means both hulls must land. `fleet.affinity_sets` now bundles each habitat 1:1 with an ECLSS unit on one hull (anti-affinity still spreads the pairs), and the `life_support_closed` gate requires the habitat with the 1,000-sol clock on both. Precedent: every closed-loop demo in history ran inside a sealed volume (NASA LMLSTP, ISS ECLSS chamber testing, MELiSSA, Lunar Palace 1); inflatables need their own endurance run (BEAM 2016).

rows.idea: `habitat_module` stowed volume = deployed volume (300 m³)
rows.why_where_recorded: The workbook-era component carried deployed volume as its stowed figure. The live baseline now flies `habitat_inflatable` (75 m³ stowed → ~300 m³ deployed, TransHab/B330 ~4× compression) in every window from 2031 on — which returned the 11th ship at 2035 (volume-bound no more) and, via the ≥2× growth rule, reset 2037 to 20 ships (fleet 394 → 392). `habitat_module` survives only inside the frozen workbook-port fixtures, which pin the original spreadsheet math and are never re-anchored. (Ship counts quoted are as of this 2026-07-13 resolution; the 2026-07-14 blue-sky/no-air-freight re-baselines superseded them.)

rows.idea: Per-ship packing checks raw volume ≤ bay, while the batch check applies packing efficiency
rows.why_where_recorded: Two volume standards coexist: the packer admits raw ≤ 614 m³/ship; the budget batch check divides by 0.65 first. The stricter batch check is what surfaces warnings. Unifying them is future work.

rows.idea: 6th ship on the 2033 fuel-factory window for redundancy
rows.why_where_recorded: The window already meets the single-fault standard: capability is backstopped by window-0's pre-deployed pilot chain (L1-LOG-02 passes), and the ~30% propellant-gate margin absorbs one lost ship's ~20% (1,826→1,525 t > 1,400). Adding a ship carries mass we don't need to meet our stated single-fault bar. The two-ship-loss cliff (~1,224 t) is beyond that bar; it's C2's probabilistic-loss target.

rows.idea: Production-margin loss requirement (gate must clear under one fuel-factory ship loss)
rows.why_where_recorded: Formalizing "propellant gate survives a ship loss" as a machine-checked requirement needs the campaign re-run with a ship dropped — the same simulation C2's probabilistic-EDL pass builds. Verified by hand for now (holds); requirement to follow.

rows.idea: Crew at the *second* window (~52 months after first landing)
rows.why_where_recorded: Their timeline assumes gates retire on first attempt. Ours holds crew to banked propellant, the completed 1,000-sol ECLSS demo, and the return demo in flight — crew arrives at the third window (2035) even in the blue-sky plan. COMPARATIVES 'where we differ'.

rows.idea: Solar-first surface power (mass-produced panels, oversize the farm)
rows.why_where_recorded: After adopting Handmer's survival-vs-production load split (which un-rigged our storm math), the trade is honest: solar is lighter but accepts months of production halt in a global storm. Fission stays baseline for the crewed era; `solar_only` scenario carries the alternative. PROVENANCE §2.

rows.idea: Split cargo/crew missions — cargo pre-deployed one synod ahead
rows.why_where_recorded: DRA 5.0's split-mission architecture is our one-window rule: hardware lands, proves itself, and only then does the next commitment ride on it. The blue-sky fly-with doctrine trims *what* pre-lands (bridge, pilots), never the proof-before-commit ordering.

rows.idea: Nuclear-thermal propulsion and DRA's crew-of-6 mission shape
rows.why_where_recorded: We assume Starship-class chemical transport and settlement-scale crews (12+); DRA's transit architecture is a different program. Only its surface doctrine (split missions, nuclear power, ISRU descope option) carries over.

rows.idea: BVAD's full ECLSS/logistics sizing model
rows.why_where_recorded: We take the metabolic floor verbatim (0.800/0.895/3.217 kg per person-day, Tier B) and the crop-area bounds; the rest of BVAD's system-by-system logistics model collapses into per-group spares fractions. Sharpening that is the `informed_spares` lineage. PROVENANCE §3.

rows.idea: Sizing habitat capacity at the bare NHV floor (~29 m³/person)
rows.why_where_recorded: The same study's 76.5 m³/person *pressurized* standard (NHV + systems + stowage) is the honest capacity divisor — the floor overstates capacity ~2.6×. This exact error shipped once ('room for 60') and was corrected in the story essay; all capacity math now uses 76.5. inputs/city.json `pressurized_volume_m3`.

rows.idea: Vazquez Pombo's 30–200 kWe/person 'ISRU' power band
rows.why_where_recorded: Their 'ISRU' is life-support-scale, NOT a propellant factory — conflating the two understates fuel-plant power ~10×. We adopt only the subsistence curve (25 → 17 kWe/person at 100+) and size the propellant chain explicitly from chemistry. inputs/city.json note.

rows.idea: Molten regolith electrolysis as the regolith-refinery process basis
rows.why_where_recorded: Regolith → oxygen + ferro-alloys, NASA-demonstrated in vacuum Dec 2024. Anchors *what the refinery is* (Tier B process); its 12 t / 200 kW catalog row stays Tier D. PROVENANCE §6.

rows.idea: Sabatier → Fischer-Tropsch → polyethylene as the polymer-plant basis
rows.why_where_recorded: CO₂ → ethylene → HDPE piggybacks the fuel plant's chemistry; products are film/liners/pipe/printer feedstock, plus PE-regolith composite radiation shielding. Anchors the Gen-2 closure tier. PROVENANCE §6.

rows.idea: Industrial-closure generations with an electronics cliff
rows.why_where_recorded: Import decay is keyed to closure generations (10 → 4 → 2 → 0.8 → 0.2 t/person-yr); incomplete electronics closure grows imports *exponentially*, which is why the fab is the last pilot (2039) and chips close last of all. inputs/city.json `import_decay_t_per_person_year`.

rows.idea: Salotti's time-budget labor mechanism behind the 110-person floor
rows.why_where_recorded: We adopt the 110 threshold as a population gate; his underlying labor model (hours available vs required, cooperation sharing factor) is not modeled. A real labor budget would also price how many robots substitute for how many settlers — future work.

rows.idea: Integrated sealed-volume life-support demonstration (no bare-ECLSS demos)
rows.source: NASA LMLSTP 1997; ISS chamber testing; BEAM 2016
rows.why_where_recorded: A loop is only demonstrable inside a sealed volume, and the integrated failure modes are the ones that matter. L2-LS-04 pins it; `fleet.affinity_sets` packs each habitat+ECLSS pair on one hull; the 1,000-sol clock runs on both articles.

rows.idea: MOXIE as the ISRU reality anchor
rows.why_where_recorded: 122 g of O₂ total at 6–12 g/hr is the entire flight heritage of Mars ISRU — ~9 orders of magnitude below our 2033 plant. It is why every ISRU component mass stays Tier D, why commissioning ramps at 0.6, and why the oxygen-only fallback (MOXIE chemistry) exists. PROVENANCE §2b/§6.

rows.idea: RedWater Rodwell rate anchor vs our water plant
rows.why_where_recorded: Source-verification pass against the primary Honeybee/Zacny publications: RedWater at TRL-6 produces ~169 kg/h at ~0.45 kWh/kg end-to-end — our unit’s rate matches, and Rodwell production is power-limited, so rate = power/energy is the right physics. The 1 t/10 days figure was a low-power operating point in a secondary summary. Water energy re-anchored 0.3 → 0.45 kWh/kg end-to-end (program-neutral: electrolysis binds every window; even 2× the anchored energy moves nothing). Excavation anchored to the RASSOR 2.0 design law, conservative at any ore grade. PROVENANCE §2b.

rows.idea: Handmer's make-vs-buy threshold (<$100/kg → always make locally)
rows.why_where_recorded: The endpoints of his curve (10 t → ~20 kg/person-yr) are the city ramp's import decay; the decision *rule* itself (a per-item make/buy economic threshold) is unmodeled — it would turn closure stages from presence-gates into economics. Natural successor to ratio-based closure.

rows.idea: Kinetic penetrators + orbital radar as the fast water-confirmation path
rows.why_where_recorded: Steel spears released on approach + Marslink-class radar retire `water_confirmed` in ~30 sols vs ~200 drilling — cheaper, faster, and loss-tolerant (doubled at window 0 since the no-air-freight top-up). The 2033 factory gate keys on it, with drilling as the fallback path.

rows.idea: Penetrator strike CONOPS: carry to orbit, deorbit on command (not release-on-approach)
rows.why_where_recorded: Scheduling beats blind timing: rods ride to the 1-sol survey ellipse and deorbit 1–2/sol (~sols 5–20), each strike timed to an observer pass. Design sketch: ~20 rods/set at 100–150 kg (steel body, tungsten nose), impact ~3.5–4 km/s — still craters through 1–3 m overburden; plume spectroscopy is opportunistic because fresh-ice craters testify for weeks (HiRISE precedent). Deconfliction near the base: **~10 km keep-out** sized by ejecta ballistics (~3 km at 100 m/s in 0.38 g), near-field ground truth assigned to flight-1's drilling prospectors / far-field to the rods, and strike ground-tracks oriented so along-track dispersion (the dominant failure) points away from assets. Bonus: each strike is a calibrated seismic source for base-region subsurface structure. The tool has no spatial dimension (see orbital-assets simplification row) — this lives as doctrine in docs/CONOPS.md, not the planner.

rows.idea: Window-0 orbital constellation: architecture and self-deployment
rows.why_where_recorded: The 6 t `orbital_radar_set` ≈ 1 areostationary relay (~2 t: continuous base–Earth link, EDL telemetry for every arriving fleet, ascent/TEI witness for the Jan-2035 receipt flight) + 3–4 polar SAR/ultraspectral smallsats (~600–800 kg) for the shallow-ice mapping the legacy fleet physically cannot do (SHARAD is blind in the top ~10 m; CRISM is dead; MRO/Odyssey/MEx will be 25–30 yr old). Deployment: dispenser separation on hyperbolic approach (MSL-ballast heritage, zero Δv cost to the ships — they never enter orbit); each sat self-captures chemically (~0.9 km/s to a 1-sol resonant ellipse: periapsis over the site every sol during the 30-sol survey) then aerobrakes to circular polar mapping orbit after the gate closes (MRO/TGO heritage). A shared OTV was **rejected**: the polar-science vs equatorial-relay plane change costs more than self-propulsion saves. Satellite count/design remain Tier D.

rows.idea: Orbital segment is flown once and never refreshed
rows.why_where_recorded: The constellation flies once, at window 0, and is never reinforced through 2041: one dispenser on one hull until release (single-point before deployment), ~5–7 yr smallsat design life ageing out right as the village arrives (2037), and by the settlement 1,112 people ride a 14-year-old never-refreshed orbital layer while bandwidth and EDL-support demand scale with population and fleet size. Candidates: a replacement/growth set every other window (2035 and 2039 fleets have mass margin) and promoting orbital assets to a real mission type (see the simplification row). Deferred pending the constellation-sizing decision; the CONOPS-2 (rotation/city era) review is the natural venue.

rows.idea: Return-flight ground segment: hulls-as-tank-farm, ascent pad, autonomous pre-flight
rows.why_where_recorded: Banked methalox stores in landed one-way hulls (~1,200 t tankage each; three hulls cover everything banked through 2035), so no dedicated storage tanks fly; the plant's reliquefaction loop manages farm boil-off (energy already in the liquefaction row). One landing pad is sintered to launch grade at debris standoff from the base, with an insulated cryo transfer run from the farm. Demonstrator pre-flight: year-ahead hull selection, robotic TPS survey with tile replacement, engine chill/spin-prime, a short static fire (also the pad's qualification event), autonomous countdown with Earth veto under light delay, and a dust-weather launch commit from the constellation. The receipt flight qualifies this same ground segment for the crew's 2037 departure. No new catalog hardware needed (sintering rigs, robots, plant cryo equipment); plume-ejecta standoff distances remain unquantified Tier D doctrine, an open item for the spatial layer.

rows.idea: Top-down fleet sizing (ships computed from cargo, not declared)
rows.why_where_recorded: Ships/window = max(cargo at <=90% fleet mass, cumulative >=2x growth floor), computed by the planner (fleet.target_fill=0.90). Replaces hand-declared counts + a check. Self-corrects on any mass change; resolved the fill-drift churn the literature-review re-baseline caused. Fleet went 322 -> 316 (5-11-16-32-94-158).

rows.idea: Water production rate ~3.6 t/day/unit (power-derived)
rows.why_where_recorded: Optimistic by 1-2 orders of magnitude. Water is process/grade-limited, not power-limited (model was wrong in kind). Re-baselined to a grade-keyed extraction cap: clean-ice Rodwell ~0.4 t/day/unit (NASA CRREL model) as baseline; ~4 units/load; crew-2035 SURVIVES. garden_regolith scenario (~29 kg/day/unit, NASA WER) starves the chain and slips crew -- the downside the minable-ice gate rules out. PROVENANCE 2b-water. Supersedes the premature 2026-07-20 'resolved' row.

rows.idea: Ice: 'present' vs 'minable' (site grade determines everything)
rows.why_where_recorded: Mars ice is bimodal: mid-latitude massive/excess ice >90% pure, 1-2 m deep (Dundas 2018/2021; SWIM/Nature Astro 2020; Arcadia-class) vs equatorial hydrated 'garden' regolith 1-8%. Orbital data proves ice EXISTS regionally; minability (grade/depth/form/extent at the plant site) needs ground truth -- the flight-1 confidence ladder (orbital->penetrator->drill->pilot plant). `water_confirmed` hardened from 'present' to 'minable-grade confirmed'.

rows.idea: EDL success-probability curve (0.5->0.95 over synods)
rows.why_where_recorded: No empirical anchor exists above ~1 t landed (Perseverance 1,026 kg record); best-credentialed large-lander concept tops out ~28 t (NASA blunt-body SRP, AIAA 2016-0219); Braun & Manning document a real physics discontinuity ('high-mass entry doesn't close at Mars'). Our 100 t is a published planning value (New Space 2022), not physically validated. The 0.5->0.95 ramp keeps the right humility but its SHAPE may be a step function (unlocked once one flight proves the architecture), not a smooth per-synod climb. Kept Tier D; do not upgrade.

rows.idea: Consumables floor 4.912 kg/person-day
rows.why_where_recorded: BVAD 0.800 kg food is NET of packaging; as-shipped is 2.39 kg (3x). Corrected in city.json: survival cache = potable water (3.217) basis, day-to-day ops = full 12.75 (hygiene, NASA PGH). Reference-only (no engine ripple); the 'years on the shelf' doc claims shrink accordingly.

rows.idea: Habitat capacity sized at 76.5 m3/person pressurized
rows.why_where_recorded: 76.5 is not a NASA standard -- it's one case study's pressurized total / 4 crew (Stromgren 2020), whose OWN recommendation is ~37 m3 NHV. Sizing basis moved to habitable_volume_m3.realistic (36.8). Reference-only in city.json.

rows.idea: Cost order-of-magnitude spread ($236-990B) is sloppy
rows.why_where_recorded: AACE 18R-97 classifies the program as Class-5 (concept), whose prescribed accuracy is -20/-50% to +30/+100%, widened 2-3x for first-of-kind -- our ~4x spread is correct, not sloppy. NASA-vs-commercial Falcon 9 estimates differed ~3x for the same vehicle, validating the near_term/spacex_internal split. Adopt AMCM $1.82M/kg as a crew-hardware sanity anchor.

rows.idea: Cryogenic zero-boil-off storage at farm scale
rows.why_where_recorded: NASA's own CFM program: no ZBO system demonstrated near farm scale (SOTA endurance ~14 h vs months-years needed); first high-capacity demo ~early 2030s -- close to our 2033/2035 windows. ~100 kW-class overhead is a defensible extrapolation but needs 700-1000 parallel 100-150 W cryocoolers, an unproven integration regime. MLI '99%' claim corrected: Mars soft-vacuum degrades MLI ~10x without a vacuum jacket. Documented; not a modeled gate.

rows.idea: Electrolysis energy 6.1 kWh/kg-H2O
rows.why_where_recorded: ISS OGA flight hardware (28-cell AOGA, 46.9 A, ~1.7 V/cell -> ~5.8 kWh/kg-O2 stack = ~5.2 kWh/kg-H2O; +system-level ~6-7) confirms our 6.1. First ISRU chain number validated against real flight hardware. PROVENANCE 2b.

blocks.md:
Findings surfaced by the two deep-research runs (`docs/_city_ramp_raw_findings.json`,
verdicts in `docs/CITY_RAMP_RESEARCH.md`) that we weighed but did not model,
chose against, or simplified — recorded so their omission is visible.

rows.idea: Per-item surface offload limit: ~30 t feasible, single 100 t payloads doubtful
rows.why_where: We assume a 100 t ship payload offloads fine; a real per-item offload cap would force cargo to be sub-divided and could change ship counts. A genuine unmodeled physical constraint — flagged for a future packing refinement, not yet acted on.

rows.idea: "Danger zone": 1,000–100,000 people is the worst range — too big to evacuate, too small to be self-sufficient
rows.why_where: A strategic risk concept, not a mass/schedule quantity. Our milestones (110 / 1,000 / 1M) bracket it but we don't score evacuation-vs-autarky exposure. Worth a lifecycle metric someday.

rows.idea: GMU agent-based minimum of 22 settlers
rows.source_verdict: technology.org / GMU preprint (Tier C, single source)
rows.why_where: We use Salotti's peer-reviewed 110 (3-0 verified) for `survival_floor`, not GMU's 22 — different framing (accident/resource survival vs labor-closure) and weaker sourcing. Noted as an alternate, lower bound.

rows.idea: NSS "reasonable self-sufficiency" = 3 capabilities (life support, local food, local habitat construction)
rows.why_where: Our `settlement_established` gates on population ≥1,000 plus the closure ladder; we do not separately score NSS's 3-capability functional test. The closure stages approximate it.

rows.idea: Per-capita material *usage* ~10× Earth on Mars
rows.why_where: Reflected implicitly in the high no-closure import figure (10 t/person/yr); we don't carry a separate Earth-vs-Mars usage multiplier.

rows.idea: Agriculture crop area (~40–50 m²/person) also closes the O₂/CO₂ loop
rows.why_where: `agri_module` models grow-lighting power and food coverage but not its life-support (O₂/CO₂) co-benefit, which would offset some ECLSS load. Conservative (understates agriculture's value).

rows.idea: Mass→population coupling: ~620,000 t cumulative import ≈ 1M people; population doubles per window
rows.why_where: Our city windows are hand-authored with explicit settler counts and fleet sizes; population is not derived from cumulative delivered mass. A mass-driven population model is the natural M7 step.

rows.idea: Maiwald specifics: ~191.5 t ISRU system, ~2.5 MW draw, ~1,263 t consumables for 12 crew/500 d
rows.why_where: The `conservative_feasibility` scenario carries the DLR *posture* (high ISRU energy, low availability, refill/chill not demonstrated) rather than these exact figures as hard anchors; they informed the direction, not specific seed values.

rows.idea: Cannon & Britt food self-sufficiency in 20–40 years
rows.why_where: We gate closure on hardware + population, not on a food-closure calendar. The 20–40 yr figure is consistent with our ~2037–2041 Gen-3 (food) closure but isn't an input.

blocks.md:
Add to this file whenever something is weighed and set aside — an entry here
is cheaper than a collaborator re-litigating it from scratch.

_comment: SOURCE OF TRUTH for this ledger. The markdown view is generated by `mars ledgers`; a freshness test enforces it. Rows are fielded objects under sections[].blocks[].rows.
